title: From Self To Others
study: A Study In The Life Of Moses
scriptureForWeek: EXODUS 2:10, ACTS 7:20-23 Exodus 2:10 Later, when the boy was older, his mother brought him back to Pharaoh’s daughter, who adopted him as her own son. The princess named him Moses, for she explained, “I lifted him out of the water.” Acts 7:20-23 "At that time Moses was born — a beautiful child in God’s eyes. His parents cared for him at home for three months. When they had to abandon him, Pharaoh’s daughter adopted him and raised him as her own son. Moses was taught all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and he was powerful in both speech and action. One day when Moses was forty years old, he decided to visit his relatives, the people of Israel.”
weekTheme: Boys play, Men learn This week, we step into a long but obscure season of Moses’ life - the time he was adopted and raised in the household of Pharaoh. The scriptures tell us that this period lasted for almost 40 years, and then he has an awakening that will launch him into a calling he cannot comprehend. It’s important to recognize this period because it was here that Moses was exposed to the religion, culture, and power that he would have to fight against in the years ahead. It reminds us that God needs to prepare us to live and serve others too, even if it’s in ways we don’t understand or expect.

days.title: A Man In Full
days.scripture: ACTS 7:20-22 20 His parents cared for him at home for three months. 21 When they had to abandon him, Pharaoh’s daughter adopted him and raised him as her own son. 22 Moses was taught all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and he was powerful in both speech and action.
days.reflection: Do you normally finish what you start? Do you normally finish what you start without being told, nagged, or reminded? Maybe your mom tells you to clean your room. Does she have to check on you to get you to finish? Do you do most of it, but not all of it? Do you do “good enough” but not all she asked? What about homework? Do you focus on it and finish when you need to, without being told? To be honest, most guys your age don’t. One of the challenges of growing up is to learn to finish and follow through on our responsibilities because we want to, not because we have to. Lots of men never finish growing up. They are half-finished men. They get some parts of manhood right but skip on others because they never bother getting around to becoming all they can be. When the nagging stops, their growth stops. This was not true of Moses. He grew all the way up. He was a man in full. His training in Pharaoh's house brought him all the way into manhood. And what he learned there was preparation for how he was going to live for others later on. He was not just learning for his own development; he was learning for others’ deliverance. This passage says 3 things that let us know Moses finished growing into a man. These are a kind of checklist to focus on as you grow up. WISDOM It says that Moses was taught "all the wisdom of the Egyptians.” Each country has those that it considers wise, those that it holds up as people we should learn from. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr was a man of wisdom around justice. Steve Jobs (the founder of Apple) was a man of wisdom and technology. Francis Collins is seen as a man of wisdom and science. Moses learned the absolute best wisdom of these kinds of leaders in Egypt. He learned the language, mythology, technology, warfare, and worship. POWERFUL IN SPEECH Speeches move the world. The right words at the right time can change everything. See if you can finish these words from famous speeches: I have a _________. (MLK) Ask not what your country can do for you, but what you __________. (JFK) Give me liberty, or give me ________. (Patrick Henry) Moses learned to become a great speaker. You will see this later on when he speaks to Pharaoh and speaks to the children of Israel. Learning to communicate well is a key to being a man. POWERFUL IN ACTION Moses knew how to get things done. This is really important for a man. Men do stuff. Men aren’t passive. They don’t sit back and see what’s going to happen; they lead out, they act, and they push. Moses was trained to get stuff done, and he got good at getting stuff done. Focus on these things. Men know how to think the right things (Wisdom). Say the right things (Words). Get the right things done (Works). Sometimes when men possess these skills they can get arrogant. They can use these gifts to build themselves up and become prideful. They can think these gifts are for themselves. But God was training Moses for others. It was not so he could be successful, but so he could be a servant of the people. God wants you to grow. And you are doing great. Keep pursuing wisdom, communication, and action. God will use it in your future.
questions: Of these three things, (wisdom, speech, and action) which of these do you think you have grown in? Which do you think you still have the most growth to do?
Who is the wisest person you know? What makes them so wise? How do you see it show up in their lives?
What is one area you normally tend to procrastinate in? Or half-finish? How can you crank that out this week?

thoughts.verse: Dear brothers and sisters, don’t be childish in your understanding of these things. Be innocent as babies when it comes to evil, but be mature in understanding matters of this kind.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you the desire to be a man who finishes things and to grow to your full potential, because you want to love and serve others.

days.title: Growth And Maturity
days.scripture: ACTS 7:20 His parents cared for him at home for three months. When they had to abandon him, Pharaoh’s daughter adopted him and raised him as her own son.
days.reflection: All guys get older; not all guys grow up. This is one of the truest things you will begin to notice as you get older. As you grow, your body changes (as you have probably figured out by now). You begin to get more hair, your muscles begin to grow, which is sometimes called “filling out.” Your voice starts to get lower. Your body is filled with testosterone. You get a bit more competitive, and think about girls in a different way - it kind of all happens at once. This is your body signaling you need to move forward in life. This is a really major part of being in a family and of the goal your life is heading toward. You are meant to learn to grow up and move out into the world as a man. Moses’ adopted mom did something important, something we may even take for granted. She RAISED HIM. Lots of parents help their sons, encourage their sons, and provide for their sons, but not all parents raise them. Sometimes they leave them stuck where they don’t get all the way into adulthood. But Moses went from a baby in a basket to a leader of Egypt, because he was raised all the way into manhood. When a baby is raised, he will go through 4 stages to reach adulthood. You will as well. The first stage is being an infant. You were an infant once, a tiny little screaming, pooping, sleeping, eating baby. You just showed up and your parents took care of you. You didn’t contribute much; you just did your thing. You made it through to the next stage. The second stage of growth is being a child. Kids live in pretty simple worlds. They do what their parents tell them, they go to school, they play, and they learn how to read, write, think, and interact with others. Their parents pay for their clothes, hobbies, school sports, everything. Though it can feel overwhelming and challenging, you actually don’t have that many responsibilities to carry or handle. The third stage is being an adolescent. This is you. Being an adolescent can be really hard. You have to learn to navigate power dynamics with other guys at school and how to interact with girls. You will probably get your first job and learn to drive. You have to develop trust and accept reasonability. It’s a lot. And you will also begin to separate from your parents a bit. You will disagree, start to explore, and develop your own preferences. You will start processing what you really think about life and faith. You will discover things for yourself. You will begin to challenge everything you have known to see what you truly think. The fourth stage is the adult. The adult accepts responsibility and finds joy in caring for others and providing for the needs of those he loves. He knows who he is, what he cares about, and what he needs to do to live the life he is called to. WARNING. Lots of folks get stuck in adolescence. They never move into adulthood and become the men they are called to be. They struggle and lag and delay development. Moses went through all 4 phases to become the man God called him to be. Keep seeking maturity. Keep seeking to be fully raised. Keep looking forward. Moses had no idea at the time as he was moving through these stages what God would do with him when he was mature, and you don’t either. You want to be a man in full, not a partial man. You are right on track. Press on.
questions: Who do you think is the most mature of your friends? Why?
Who do you think is the most immature? Why?
What do you think are a couple of traps that keep guys from fully growing up in our modern world?
Ask the person you are doing this study with when he knew he was finally an adult. What happened to let him know this? How did he handle it?

thoughts.verse: So get rid of all evil behavior. Be done with all deceit, hypocrisy, jealousy, and all unkind speech. Like newborn babies, you must crave pure spiritual milk so that you will grow into a full experience of salvation. Cry out for this nourishment, now that you have had a taste of the Lord’s kindness.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a vision of being a man in full, of being mature and wise and someone who can navigate all the hard stuff life will throw at you.

days.title: Powerful In Speech
days.scripture: ACTS 7:22 Moses was taught all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and he was powerful in both speech and action.
days.reflection: Words can change the world. Words have changed your world. Hearing “You’re really good at that” can fill you with confidence. Hearing, “You’re an idiot” can beat you down. Hearing, “You made it on the team” can fill you with joy. Hearing “You’re not invited” can fill you with sadness. Your life has probably been defined by things that have been said to you. Words are small little letters lumped together to form sentences, but they have staggering power. And you are going to say a lot of them. According to Gyles Brandreth, a British actor and Broadcast personality, the typical person will say 860,341,500 in a lifetime. Let that sink in. You are going to do a lot of talking over the course of your life. That’s the equivalent of speaking the entire 32- volume Encyclopedia Britannica 19.5 times. Or to put it another way, it’s the equivalent of reading the entire Bible, Old and New Testaments 1,110 times out loud. The verse above says that Moses was “powerful in speech.” He had the ability to communicate in such a way that stirred others to act. God made sure Moses had the training he needed to speak the truth to a whole nation. If you are going to be a man who can love others, build them up, and speak hope into their lives, you need to learn to watch your words closely. James 3:2 makes it clear that watching our words is a key priority for every guy to focus on. It says, “Indeed, we all make many mistakes. For if we could control our tongues, we would be perfect and could also control ourselves in every other way.” Moses learned to control his speech. You must too. Here are some guidelines: Boys blurt out whatever they think; men guard their words. James 1:26 says, “If you claim to be religious but don’t control your tongue, you are fooling yourself, and your religion is worthless.” Boys use words to wound; men use words to heal. Proverbs 12:18 says, “The words of the reckless pierce like swords, but the tongue of the wise brings healing. Boy talk is silly and rude; a man’s talk is controlled and appropriate. Ephesians 5:4 says, “Obscene stories, foolish talk, and coarse jokes—these are not for you. Instead, let there be thankfulness to God.” What you say can get you in trouble, beat others down, start fights, and make enemies. What you say can also grant you favor, help you make friends, navigate hard situations, and earn respect. If you are going to become a skillful man, you have to watch your words. This will take time, practice, humility, and patience, but it will be worth it. Words create worlds, even when you are young. Build a good one with what you say.
questions: Who talks the most in your family? Who talks the least?
What is one of the most encouraging things someone has said to you lately?
What is one of the most discouraging things someone has said?
You will speak about 16,000 words today. Can you think of a person who needs encouragement? Take a moment to think of something you can say to build them up.

thoughts.verse: Let your conversation be gracious and attractive so that you will have the right response for everyone.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God for help in controlling what you say. Ask him to give you boldness, restraint, and skill in how you speak to others.

days.title: Powerful In Action
days.scripture: ACTS 7:22 Moses was taught all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and he was powerful in both speech and action.
days.reflection: Men do stuff. Good stuff. Kind stuff. Helpful stuff. Godly stuff. They stop bad stuff. They fight evil stuff. They do massive amounts of stuff. Men have a bias towards action. They don’t shrink back; they don’t make excuses. They reject passivity and get after it. This may sound obvious, and even a bit harsh, but you are growing up in a world where lots of guys struggle to take action. They wrestle with being passive. You live in a world where people share opinions but rarely take action. But what you do is a better reflection of what you believe, not just the opinion you have. Opinions are cheap; actions are costly. When God wants to develop a man, he will focus on getting him to act. To act with wisdom, to act with restraint, to act with power, to act with endurance. Don’t fall into the trap of thinking that just because you have learned something you have done something. James 1:22-25 points this out: “But don’t just listen to God’s word. You must do what it says. Otherwise, you are only fooling yourselves. For if you listen to the word and don’t obey, it is like glancing at your face in a mirror. You see yourself, walk away, and forget what you look like. But if you look carefully into the perfect law that sets you free, and if you do what it says and don’t forget what you heard, then God will bless you for doing it.” The blessing comes in the doing, not just the hearing. Jesus was described in Luke’s gospel as “a prophet, powerful in word and deed before God and all the people” (Luke 24:19). Jesus was known for what he did, not just what he taught. God made sure Moses learned how to take action. It was an important part of his development. The things Moses learned in this obscure season of his life were the very things God would use later in his life. Moses learned to act, and soon he would be acting to judge Egypt, confront Pharaoh, and lead the people out of slavery and toward the promised land. Moses is remembered mainly for the actions he took, not the opinions he had. To become a man of action, you need to learn to do the next right thing, even if it seems small. Action in small areas can lead to massive action in larger areas. When your friends are sitting around talking, stand up and do something. When everyone is giving opinions, take action. Even in small things like standing up for a friend, telling the truth, or confronting someone who is lying - these things all add up. Do stuff. Clean your room, crank your homework, take out the trash, do the dishes. If you learn to develop a bias towards action now, it will serve you well in the years ahead. Our culture needs men who take action, not give options. Do stuff.
questions: Who is your favorite superhero? Why? What kind of action do they take that inspires you?
Who in your family do you think has the biggest bias toward action? How do they show it?
Can you name an area you are good at taking action in and an area you struggle to take action in?

thoughts.verse: Dear children, let’s not merely say that we love each other; let us show the truth by our actions. Our actions will show that we belong to the truth, so we will be confident when we stand before God.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you from it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment and ask God to give you a vision of being a man of action. Ask him to give you courage and strength to step up today in an area you normally shrink back in.

days.title: Wise As Serpents
days.scripture: ACTS 7:22 Moses was taught all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and he was powerful in both speech and action.
days.reflection: ake a moment and answer this question: What is the difference between wisdom and knowledge? You live at a time of history where you have access to more information than any generation that has ever lived. You don’t just have access to all the information in a local library, but all the information on the internet. So much content! So many ideas! But knowing lots of things is not the same as being wise. Being wise is about knowing how to do the right thing at the right time. It's about becoming skilled and learning to master the art of living. Moses was raised in Egypt, and Egypt had a class of leaders called the “Hakamim.” Charles Strohmer notes that “The word ‘hakam’ appears frequently in the Hebrew Bible to denote a wise and very skilled individual, often a high-level official in a royal court. ‘Hakamim’ appears many times in the Old Testament referring to high- level officials and advisers to a king. These were government officials that today we would call ambassadors, diplomats, foreign ministers, secretaries of state, international negotiators and the like.” These sound like some pretty important and powerful people. And these are the people that helped raise Moses. It would be kind of like having friends in the Whitehouse who taught you about politics, friends in the UN who taught you about international relations, and friends on Wall Street who taught you about money. Moses was taught by the smartest and most savvy men of his time. God was preparing Moses by giving him a super strong education, so he could lead with incredible skill. Now what is interesting is that the primary word for “wisdom” in Hebrew is the word “chokmah” which is derived from the same root word, Hakamim. Here is what it means for a young guy like you. God wants you to be wise. He wants you to learn to live skillfully. He wants you to learn to be able to handle things like an ambassador, a statesman, a sage, a negotiator. He wants you to be able to think and act in a clear way that helps you live skillfully in the modern world. But you have to WANT to be wise. You have to pursue wisdom. You can't just drift along. Proverbs 1:32 says, “For the waywardness of the simple will kill them, and the complacency of fools will destroy them.” Making mistakes as you grow up is normal, but making the same ones and never learning is tragic. Proverbs 26:11 warns “As a dog returns to its vomit, so a fool repeats his foolishness.” Gross, but true. Make new mistakes; grow from your old ones. At your age, it’s easy to just do whatever comes into your head without thinking, but becoming a wise man makes you think before you act. You must learn to ask yourself, “what’s the wise thing to do in this situation?” Proverbs 14:16 says, “The wise are cautious and avoid danger; fools plunge ahead with reckless confidence.” God put Moses in an environment where he learned to become wise. God prepared him with a world-class education so he could become a world-class leader. God wants you to become wise too. He wants to make you the kind of guy that others look up to for advice. The kind of guy that knows what to do in hard situations, the kind of person who can figure out what to do when others are freaking out. So ask God for wisdom. He wants to give it to you. James 1:5-6 is an awesome promise: “If you need wisdom, ask our generous God, and he will give it to you. He will not rebuke you for asking. But when you ask him, be sure that your faith is in God alone.” God prepared Moses to be the leader he needed to free his people. He made sure he was fully raised. He made sure he grew up all the way. He made sure he was wise. He made sure he could communicate well. He made sure he knew how to take decisive action. God did this because Moses was destined to help others. God wants to use you to help others, and he is preparing you. Very few guys your age are taking the time to learn and grow like you are on the Primal Path. You are making good progress. Keep going - there are really cool things ahead.
questions: Being wise is about learning lessons.
What is one thing you have learned about God this year?
What is one thing you have learned about yourself?
What is one thing you have learned about life?
What is one action step you can take to keep growing in wisdom?

thoughts.verse: So be careful how you live. Don’t live like fools, but like those who are wise. Make the most of every opportunity in these evil days. Don’t act thoughtlessly, but understand what the Lord wants you to do.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a heart of wisdom. Ask him to help you reject the foolishness that’s around you so you can be a source of help to others.

extras: WEEK 2 MANSCHOOL THE DASH MANSCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. THE DASH Take a trip to a local cemetery. Have your son walk around and notice the lives of people who lived there. Make the point that the entire life of the person is reflected in the tiny little dash between the date they were born and the date they died. (You can read more about this idea and when I took my son Nate to do this when we were doing The Primal Path in the introduction to the book The Burden is Light) Then get him to draw the date he was born and leave a dash. Ask him to think about the things he wants to see happen in his life. What sort of things might God be preparing him for? What is in his heart? What are some of the things he wants to do before he dies? What legacy does he want to leave? What experiences does he want to have? Then have a discussion about living a life of legacy by shifting from a life of regret living for himself and embracing a life of living for others. OPTION 2. WATCH Watch The Great Debaters (with Denzel Washington) It's a great film about the power of words and being “mighty in speech” to bring about change.
